_meta.description: IRC §129 Dependent Care Assistance (DCFSA) exclusion + IRC §21 Child & Dependent Care Credit (CDCTC), as amended by OBBBA (P.L. 119-21, §70404), effective TY2026. Drives /dependent-care-fsa-vs-credit-calculator. Figures are FIXED statutory amounts (not inflation-indexed on the codified text). Federal-only; state conformity out of scope. This is a NEW sibling system to obbba-deductions-2026.json — §21 is a tiered NONREFUNDABLE CREDIT and §129 is an INCOME + FICA EXCLUSION, neither of which is the MAGI-phased above-the-line DEDUCTION shape of that file.
_meta.confidence: high — §21/§129 statutory text read verbatim (law.cornell.edu); every fixture dollar re-derived vs tax-data-2026.json 2026 brackets/FICA (SS wage base $184,500).

dcfsa.qualifies: Pre-tax dependent-care exclusion capped at $7,500 per tax return ($3,750 MFS — exactly half; the historic 2:1 ratio is preserved) for TY2026+. Per RETURN, not per employee/employer: two working spouses filing jointly share ONE $7,500; excess over the cap is taxable wages. Section 125 cafeteria benefit → reduces federal income-tax wages AND FICA wages (~7.65% below the SS wage base). Limited to the lesser spouse's earned income; requires an employer DCAP.

cdctc.mfsException: IRC §21(e)(4) — a spouse living apart for the last 6 months who maintains the household is treated as unmarried and may claim it. Not modeled; the tool disallows the credit for married-filing-separately.
cdctc.qualifies: Nonrefundable credit = applicablePercent × min(qualifying expenses, cap, earned-income limit), clamped to income-tax liability. applicablePercent starts at 50%, reduced 1 pt per $2,000 over $15,000 (floor 35%; NOT joint-doubled in stage 1 — same $15,000 threshold and $2,000 steps for single AND joint), flat 35% to $75k single/$150k joint, then reduced 1 pt per $2,000 ($4,000 joint) over $75k/$150k (floor 20%). Expense cap $3,000 (1 dependent)/$6,000 (2+) is REDUCED dollar-for-dollar by the §129 exclusion (§21(c) flush). Married filers must file jointly to claim it (§21(e)(2)); MFS is generally ineligible.

interaction.capReductionRule: creditableExpenses = max(0, min(totalExpenses − fsaExclusion, cap − fsaExclusion, earnedIncomeLimit − fsaExclusion))
interaction.maxFsaZeroesCreditNote: Because the new max FSA $7,500 > the $6,000 two-child cap, maxing the FSA drives creditable expenses to $0 for ANY family size — the credit is zeroed. Under the old $5,000 FSA a two-child family kept $1,000 of credit room; that is gone.
interaction.cornerSolutionNote: Because §129 erodes the §21 cap dollar-for-dollar, total benefit is linear in the FSA amount and the optimum is a CORNER — max the FSA or skip it and take the credit. There is no smooth 'optimal split' to solve for.
interaction.agiFeedback: The FSA is excluded from gross income, so AGI is net of the FSA. Look up applicablePercent at the POST-FSA AGI in the max-FSA scenario (a lower AGI can raise the credit rate).

sources.claim: IRC §129(a)(2): dependent care assistance exclusion $7,500 ($3,750 for a separate return by a married individual); per-return limit; earned-income limited (§129(b)); requires an employer dependent-care assistance program.

IRC §21(a)(2) applicable percentage: 50% reduced (not below 35%) by 1 pt per $2,000 of AGI over $15,000, then reduced (not below 20%) by 1 pt per $2,000 ($4,000 joint) over $75,000 ($150,000 joint); §21(c) $3,000/$6,000 expense caps reduced by §129 exclusions; §21(d) earned-income limit; §21(e)(2)/(4) joint-return requirement; nonrefundable.

IRC §21(c) flush language: the $3,000/$6,000 dollar limit shall be reduced by the aggregate amount excludable from gross income under section 129 for the taxable year (the no-double-dip cap reduction).
OBBBA (P.L. 119-21) §70404 amended both §129 and §21, effective for taxable years beginning after 2025-12-31; permanent; first dependent-care benefit update in decades.
$7,500 DCFSA + 50% CDCTC top rate, effective 2026, expense caps unchanged, credit nonrefundable, no-double-dip cap reduction — employer-benefits summary.
The CDCTC is nonrefundable, so it gives little or no benefit to low-income families who owe little or no federal income tax — the DCFSA's FICA saving is not liability-limited.
Maxing the new $7,500 DCFSA can leave $0 of credit-eligible expenses because it exceeds the $6,000 two-child cap (worked reduction example).
IRS Publication 503, Child and Dependent Care Expenses — earned-income limit, the $3,000/$6,000 expense limits, and the coordination with employer-provided dependent care benefits (Form 2441).
IRS Topic no. 602, Child and Dependent Care Credit — qualifying-individual rules (under 13 or disabled), joint-return requirement for married filers, nonrefundable credit.
2026 Social Security wage base $184,500 at 6.2%; Medicare 1.45% (no wage cap); Additional Medicare 0.9% over the filing-status threshold — used for the DCFSA's FICA saving.
